<@U8A5MS6R1> Yeah, I'd started to suspect we were talking past each other. Sorry if I started on irrelevant tangents.

I think your description of the nature and challenges of developing systems is pretty accurate, and it's definitely a problem we need to solve. I'm sure relevant theories exist, but I don't know much about them yet.

One thing I think is true: Because parts of a system will inevitably be developed using different programming languages or frameworks, any kind of verification has to be done in a language-independent way. This means parts of a system need a common means to "advertise" how they behave to one another. There's a concept that already exists for this: Interface Description Languages (<https://bit.ly/2ImaUf7>). Huge companies like Google and Facebook are using these languages to bolt their systems together already. Some of these languages, like ProtoBuf, have a way to specify forwards and backwards compatibility so that you can upgrade pieces of a system without the other pieces breaking.

<@U8A5MS6R1> Yeah, I'd started to suspect we were talking past each other. Sorry if I started on irrelevant tangents.

I think your description of the nature and challenges of developing systems is pretty accurate, and it's definitely a problem we need to solve. I'm sure relevant theories exist, but I don't know much about them yet.

One thing I think is true: Because parts of a system will inevitably be developed using different programming languages or frameworks, any kind of verification has to be done in a language-independent way. This means parts of a system need a common means to "advertise" how they behave to one another. There's a concept that already exists for this: Interface Description Languages (<https://bit.ly/2ImaUf7>). Huge companies like Google and Facebook are using these languages to bolt their systems together already. Some of these languages, like ProtoBuf, have a way to specify forwards and backwards compatibility so that you can upgrade pieces of a system without the other pieces breaking.
There's also OpenAPI too (<https://bit.ly/2OVAcDw>) but I'm yet to fully understand it.
There's also OpenAPI too (<https://bit.ly/2OVAcDw>) but I'm yet to fully understand it.
This is why the problem is nuanced.
This is why the problem is nuanced.

I don't think there's anything irrelevant in the above conversation, it's more about settling on a shared context. (If anything I took your original thread and threw in a different context.)

The solutions like protobuf, thrift, avro, etc. are good engineering solutions and they *build up* from the existing foundations. My belief (shared with some others here I think) is that the foundations themselves could use some rethinking. This means questioning some well established, fundamental ideas such as what is an OS? Do we need one? What belongs in an OS? E.g. should it provide a richer communication mechanism than 'byte streams'? Should it provide a richer persistence model than 'files'? Should it include a type system? Could it be distributed by default? Should it be virutalized by default? If we change the foundational substrate, it is possible the structures we build with it (our programming languages+environments, build systems, applications, runtimes, package systems, databases...) also look very different. Perhaps some problems that we solve in the higher layers and introduce complexity, could end up being very easy in this imaginary world. That said, it is extremely useful to invent and explore on top the existing foundations as well, to find the 'good ideas'. So there's a lot of avenues to explore wrt 'future of programming'.

The solutions like protobuf, thrift, avro, etc. are good engineering solutions and they *build up* from the existing foundations. My belief (shared with some others here I think) is that the foundations themselves could use some rethinking. This means questioning some well established, fundamental ideas such as what is an OS? Do we need one? What belongs in an OS? E.g. should it provide a richer communication mechanism than 'byte streams'? Should it provide a richer persistence model than 'files'? Should it include a type system? Could it be distributed by default? Should it be virutalized by default? If we change the foundational substrate, it is possible the structures we build with it (our programming languages+environments, build systems, applications, runtimes, package systems, databases...) also look very different. Perhaps some problems that we solve in the higher layers and introduce complexity, could end up being very easy in this imaginary world. That said, it is extremely useful to invent and explore on top the existing foundations as well, to find the 'good ideas'. So there's a lot of avenues to explore wrt 'future of programming'.

> What belongs in an OS? E.g. should it provide a richer communication mechanism than 'byte streams'? Should it provide a richer persistence model than 'files'?

I agree re-thinking some fundamentals can be necessary. I think this challenge is better phrased as a problem of _abstractions_ though. For example, all of computing comes down to bytes, but abstractions like files, data types, and interface description languages make thinking about bytes unnecessary. I think the "future of programming" is about finding the best set of paradigms and abstractions for building systems.

(Thinking too much about reinventing _operating systems_ can be dangerous. Operating systems are low-level behemoths, and realistically we don't have the resources, community, or industry agreement that is necessary for doing that kind of work. The right set of abstractions will be able to run atop any existing operating system and computer hardware.)

> What belongs in an OS? E.g. should it provide a richer communication mechanism than 'byte streams'? Should it provide a richer persistence model than 'files'?

I agree re-thinking some fundamentals can be necessary. I think this challenge is better phrased as a problem of _abstractions_ though. For example, all of computing comes down to bytes, but abstractions like files, data types, and interface description languages make thinking about bytes unnecessary. I think the "future of programming" is about finding the best set of paradigms and abstractions for building systems.

(Thinking too much about reinventing _operating systems_ can be dangerous. Operating systems are low-level behemoths, and realistically we don't have the resources, community, or industry agreement that is necessary for doing that kind of work. The right set of abstractions will be able to run atop any existing operating system and computer hardware.)

Agree 100% with your comment about this being a problem of finding the best set of abstractions. Yes there's a risk in reinventing things too. If we build a new substrate, it should be widely portable and should run atop the existing behemoths. Then when everyone is on the new stuff, we switch out the behemoths underneath with something compact :wink:.

Some random thoughts after reading it once:

1. Weren’t the first computers also more like the Smalltalk system then, when we still set state directly, before being able to read in serialized programs from punch cards, and later (paper or magnetic) tapes? The point being: maybe the serialized aspect isn’t as important as a distinction?

2. I see what you’re doing to prevent premature definitions, but ultimately just “system” feels to unspecific. Everything is a system these days. Are we talking about interactive (non-)deterministic systems here? Do these properties help us to better define what we’re designing? Surely bringing up UI locks you into a more specific type of system. But maybe UI doesn’t have to be involved and are we then talking about a different kind of system?

3. I always felt that there is a step we take when we move from a program that is basically just a more complex function (start-input-calculate-output-done) or collection of computations to something that is based on a core loop, which is sometimes also called a simulation. There is certainly a difference between these and it might or might not be important for what you want to get at.
To me it feels we left “programs” behind a long time ago and misuse the name for things that aren’t really programs anymore, and that’s just confusing everyone.

4. I wholeheartedly agree that this is about “designing systems” and that “programming” as in modifying a serial representation of instructions with symbols is just one way of designing these systems.

Thinking about my point 3 above more I noticed that I used a very imperative way of expressing it with computations and loops, and I was just wondering what a more declarative/functional way of describing that difference between program and system as I defined it (probably wrongly) would be?

<@U5STGTB3J> Also interestingly, functions are easily composable, but we don't really know how to compose reactive programs. That's because composing functions merely gives you another function, but composing reactive programs gives you a concurrent system!

> Along a different direction, we can also consider replacing the write-then-run program idea (this is the ‘submit a paper’ model) with an incremental model where the programmer successively provides more detail about the desired behavior and a constraint solving system refines the behavior (this is the ‘have a conversation’ model).

Also was thinking about this, like very much this idea.

Human manually takes input from certain source. Then manually processes that concrete input (as we often do in repl). Environment records it as a scenario.

Having at least two scenarios environment tries to generalize them and produce a program, which tries to process similar input.

If it derived several ways process some piece of input, it will ask human for assistance. Ask to make a choice of correct one, or solve it manually.

So program becomes just a collection of recorded interactions that were manually conducted by human.

> Along a different direction, we can also consider replacing the write-then-run program idea (this is the ‘submit a paper’ model) with an incremental model where the programmer successively provides more detail about the desired behavior and a constraint solving system refines the behavior (this is the ‘have a conversation’ model).

So program becomes just a collection of recorded interactions that were manually conducted by human.
But I have no understanding how hard it would be to produce that kind of generalization of scenarios. Especially if it involves several inputs and outputs.

Very interesting thread! When talking about systems, there’s the additional challenge that everything is fractal and systems contain other systems or form another system. When we talk about composing systems, we might have something like Unix pipes in mind, where we chain certain executables which can be written in different programming languages. And it is one thing to think more about the qualities these executables need to have to make this possible. But then this only works because there is the Unix philosophy and concept of pipes and filters and byte streams, which in itself is a (more abstract) system that has been designed to make this all work. 

It’s hard to figure out which level of abstraction we are currently thinking about as there will always be layers above and beneath it (e.g. the _What belongs into an OS?_ question). It almost feels like the fundamental problem to me: pinning our thoughts and ideas to a certain level, so we can start experimenting and noticing when we make assumptions or specify requirements for the layers above and beneath.

And it is one thing to think more about the qualities these executables need to have to make this possible. But then this only works because there is the Unix philosophy and concept of pipes and filters and byte streams, which in itself is a (more abstract) system that has been designed to make this all work. 

It’s hard to figure out which level of abstraction we are currently thinking about as there will always be layers above and beneath it (e.g. the _What belongs into an OS?_ question). It almost feels like the fundamental problem to me: pinning our thoughts and ideas to a certain level, so we can start experimenting and noticing when we make assumptions or specify requirements for the layers above and beneath.

Yes! Everything seems like a system fractal. Every level is similar to every other level in some respects - definitely in the respect that each level is exactly as powerful as a Turing machine. What if we pin ourselves to an arbitrary level X - doesn't matter what it is - and we define an abstract model. Further, we design it such that if we go to Level X-1 or Level X+1, we find different details but with exactly the same abstract model. This is the idea of proof by induction and in recursive design ("make the parts have the same power as the whole" Bob Barton via Alan Kay). So if we define how Level X things compose and become Level X+1, but also require that the Level X+1 thus produced shares the same abstract model as X, we can now automatically go to Level X+2, employing the same composition rules. I don't know if this is feasible or even resonable, but it certainly is an idea that I find very interesting. There are faults here too, e.g. the 'single model fallacy' if there is such a term.

> We could imagine a system where each object has internal space which holds a cluster of inner objects, and so on. This introduces a recursive aspect in the design.

That right there is my current obsession. In fact, the whole "Axes of Freedom" section is a pretty complete list of the things I'm actively working on — nested structure, interactive refinement, managed time. I enjoyed the post quite a lot. I don't feel there was a lack of clarity around the audience (to me, the audience was clearly _you_ — this was a statement of purpose, not a call to arms). I'm interested in seeing what steps you take to pursue this vision.

My first reaction to this was.. "I'd rather just drag vertices around, thank you very much."

Then after giving it a moment to sink in, I realized.. this is actually quite powerful. In fact, it perfectly matches the abstraction behind SVG paths, so you could create this UI atop a traditional points-and-edges / bezier based vector graphics system as an alternative mode of interacting with the graphic. This approach is more structured, whereas the points-and-edges approach is more expressive. This might be a neat way to tie a toolset like Sketch or Illustrator into a structured graphical environment like Charticulator (<https://charticulator.com>) or Drawing Dynamic Visualizations (<https://vimeo.com/66085662>).

My first reaction to this was.. "I'd rather just drag vertices around, thank you very much."

Then after giving it a moment to sink in, I realized.. this is actually quite powerful. In fact, it perfectly matches the abstraction behind SVG paths, so you could create this UI atop a traditional points-and-edges / bezier based vector graphics system as an alternative mode of interacting with the graphic. This approach is more structured, whereas the points-and-edges approach is more expressive. This might be a neat way to tie a toolset like Sketch or Illustrator into a structured graphical environment like Charticulator (<https://charticulator.com>) or Drawing Dynamic Visualizations (<https://vimeo.com/66085662>).

{aside: You could also tie this in to 3d graphics. The key detail that allows for this tying-in is that turtle graphics creates paths with a defined start and end (obviously), but that's the same way SVG-like vectors (including Flash) and 3d polygonal graphics are represented. You might look at a complex vector or mesh with lots of T intersections and think "that's free-form", but (with the exception of Figma [<https://www.figma.com>]), that's actually not the case. Points in a path are ordered according to a fairly strict ruleset that could be cleanly mapped to a turtle graphic representation.}

Points in a path are ordered according to a fairly strict ruleset that could be cleanly mapped to a turtle graphic representation.}

text: CSG is similar to turtle graphics, yep. But I'm not sure there'd be the same nice, clean bidirectional mapping between the artistic representation (polygon meshes) and structured/semantic representation (procedural solids).

CSG is similar to turtle graphics, yep. But I'm not sure there'd be the same nice, clean bidirectional mapping between the artistic representation (polygon meshes) and structured/semantic representation (procedural solids).

When I, or others, say they want to reinvent “programming,” a next thought is: how much of programming? The diversity of computational tasks is rather large. I’ve attempted to build my own diagram of the centers where human motivations and technical concerns tend to simultaneously cluster. (The central “computer” is not a cluster, but a representation of the ubiquitous cross-cutting tooling). If anyone has any knowledge of pre-existing “taxonomies” of computer science, or any thoughts on the diagram, I’d be happy to hear it.

When I, or others, say they want to reinvent “programming,” a next thought is: how much of programming? The diversity of computational tasks is rather large. I’ve attempted to build my own diagram of the centers where human motivations and technical concerns tend to simultaneously cluster. (The central “computer” is not a cluster, but a representation of the ubiquitous cross-cutting tooling). If anyone has any knowledge of pre-existing “taxonomies” of computer science, or any thoughts on the diagram, I’d be happy to hear it.

When working with CSG tools within a 3d editor that also offers free-form modelling (eg: 3DS Max, Maya, Modo, etc x 100), you run into the problem where you eventually need to destroy your procedural stack in order to achieve an artistic goal. That sucks. It'd be awfully nice to be able to represent free-form changes at the same semantic level as constructive/procedural changes. I'm sure that's been done, I just don't think it's filtered up to the level of 3d art tools.

I'm sure that's been done, I just don't think it's filtered up to the level of 3d art tools.
</rant about csg>
</rant about csg>

A paper was just present at ICFP this week demonstrating a system that can take a mesh and infer a set of solid modeling operations. (Union, intersection, difference, scaling, translation of primitive geometric solids.) <https://homes.cs.washington.edu/~cnandi/docs/icfp18-cr.pdf>

You can play with it here: <http://incarnate.uwplse.org/docs.html>

attachments.title: Reincarnate
attachments.text: Reincarnate is a project out of UW PLSE that aims to improve current 3D printing techniques using approaches from the programming languages community. We focus on design synthesis, optimizing compilation, and runtime monitoring. Now we have a compilation/synthesis tool availiable through our site allowing you to test out our 3D printing pipeline.

text: You can play with it here: <http://incarnate.uwplse.org/docs.html>

text: This is neat — a different categorization than I'm used to, giving a different lens on the problem space. For example, asking, "Where does HCI fit into this taxonomy?" produces an answer of "To varying degrees, in varying ways, in every bubble." Coolness.

This is neat — a different categorization than I'm used to, giving a different lens on the problem space. For example, asking, "Where does HCI fit into this taxonomy?" produces an answer of "To varying degrees, in varying ways, in every bubble." Coolness.

I bristle a little bit at "The Web" sitting under "Consumptive", considering that I spend an awful lot of my time _making arty stuff_ for the web. There's a _producer_ behind all of those listed examples of consumables. It's definitely a bidirectional medium, arguably (in)distinct from the "Creative" bubble. But, I take your point — there are many more consumers than producers.

HCI should matter to each, as each cluster should be oriented around some cluster of human motivation.

"Human motivation" is a good framing for this!

<@U5STGTB3J> - great points.
>  “system” feels to unspecific. Everything is a system these days.
I agree. Even a program can be thought of as a 'system'.  I struggle with how to phrase this better. I could start with 'just physical hardware' but I do want to include the idea of 'software', even while not adding any notions of what the software looks like.

> I always felt that there is a step we take when we move from a program that is basically just a more complex function (start-input-calculate-output-done) or collection of computations to something that is based on a core loop, which is sometimes also called a simulation.

Interesting.. the later model can simulate any system so it's a virtual system in that sense.

> a more declarative/functional way of describing that difference between program and system as I defined it (probably wrongly) would be?

I struggled with precise definitions too and then gave up because these are all made up categories in the study of artificial machines. To me the system *provides* the idea of a program. The program is operated on from the 'outside' - you write it, run it etc. so there is other stuff outside of the program that lets you do this - these are parts of the system outside the program.

Instead of definitions, what I'm trying to identify are the typical connotations associated with 'a program' - e.g. the fact that it has two 'views': the static structural internal view in the programming language and the dynamic interactive run time view (in the UI language?). Another one is that whenever you run it, it starts afresh and the internal state is reset. Identifying these might indicate room to explore for whatever other abstractions we design.

<@UCKRZS3DZ> There are some interesting, partially overlapping ideas in constraint programming and programming by example (PbE). A fascinating space to explore no doubt. See also the recent most demo by Jonathan Edwards: <https://vimeo.com/274771188>
